problem_statement: After a planned restart, one branch of a public library's automated return sorter began sending most books to the staffed exception lane. The library must restore routing without risking book damage or silently changing catalog records.

observations.statement: Sorter S2's exception-lane rate rose from 2.1% to 68.4% immediately after the 04:30 restart; adjacent sorter S1 remained between 1.8% and 2.4%.
RFID reader traces from 120 diverted books contain valid tag frames and catalog identifiers, with signal strength matching successful reads from the prior day.
Maintenance staff passed belt-speed, gate-actuation, and bin-presence checks on S2, and ten manually commanded gates moved to the requested positions without hesitation.
The active S2 site profile has strip_prefix_chars set to 0; the signed profile approved for that branch and the still-working S1 profile both use 3 for the same catalog namespace.
observations.provenance: Configuration controller snapshot and approved branch profile

observations.statement: The restart deployed no application or firmware release; signed executable and library inventories match S1 and the previous S2 run.
observations.provenance: Deployment ledger and device inventory attestation

observations.statement: Replaying 200 archived tag frames against an isolated routing evaluator reproduced 137 exceptions with the active profile and 4 with the signed branch profile; destination bins for the other 196 matched their historical outcomes.
The catalog authority reports no bulk identifier changes, and the same sampled books routed normally through S1 during a controlled manual feed.
observations.provenance: Catalog audit plus circulation supervisor witness log

observations.statement: The safety boundary kept S2 in single-book maintenance mode with staff at every gate, forbade firmware and catalog writes, and required immediate stop on any destination mismatch.

After restoring the signed branch profile and restarting only the routing evaluator, 600 witnessed returns produced a 2.0% exception rate with zero wrong-bin events; the next seven days remained within the established 1.7% to 2.5% band.
observations.provenance: Post-change acceptance run and seven-day operations report
